hardChecks.label: Citation source ids
hardChecks.expectation: Every cited source id exists in the source packet

hardChecks.expectation: estimatedUsd is present when pricing is configured

hardChecks.expectation: Input, cached-input, and output token counts are coherent

hardChecks.expectation: Provider, model, and prompt version are present

hardChecks.expectation: Judge prompt omits eval-only object keys and holdout tuning fields

hardChecks.label: Judge claim coverage
hardChecks.expectation: LLM judge returns exactly one judgment for every briefing claim

claimJudgments.claimText: Reviewer rotation reduced median review wait time from 19 hours to 13 hours, but P90 remains 46 hours due to complex changes waiting on the same two senior reviewers.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 states median wait improved from 19 to 13 hours after reviewer rotation and P90 is still 46 hours because complex changes wait for the same two senior reviewers.

claimJudgments.claimText: Docs and configuration changes moved faster after rotation, while cross-service/shared-service changes still experienced long waits due to scarce/overloaded expert reviewers.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 says documentation and configuration reviews moved quickly after rotation, while shared-service/cross-service changes still waited on scarce reviewers. “Overloaded expert reviewers” is a reasonable paraphrase of the same two senior/scarce reviewers bottleneck.

claimJudgments.claimText: Queue tail latency is primarily an ownership-routing problem (especially for shared services), not only a staffing problem.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 explicitly recommends treating tail latency as an ownership-routing problem, not only a staffing problem, and identifies shared/cross-service changes as the tail-latency driver.

claimJudgments.claimText: Reviewers support auto-assignment for routine changes but distrust automated approvals, especially when tests are flaky or ownership is unclear.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 states reviewers like auto-assignment for routine changes but distrust bot approvals when tests are flaky or ownership is unclear.

claimJudgments.claimText: Reviewers want risk labels that explain why a change is safe to fast-track (ownership metadata, test health, file categories) and want the label to be challengeable before merge.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 says reviewers asked for risk labels explaining why a change is safe to fast-track, including ownership metadata, test health, and file categories, and that the label should be challengeable before merge.

recommendationJudgment.explanation: The recommendation is well aligned with the packet: it targets P90 latency through ownership routing (S1, S5), limits automation to organization/routing rather than approval bypass (S3, S4, S6), constrains fast-track to docs/config with healthy tests and challengeable labels (S2, S6), and requires owning-team review for cross-service changes to avoid repeat incidents (S3, S6). Some implementation details such as “CODEOWNERS” and explicit escalation are operational extrapolations, but they are consistent with the evidence and not overclaimed.

rubricEvidence: Fake demo variant score for visual rehearsal: overall 0.94.
